comments: This paper proposes a practical pipeline to harvest and analyze social media content for open innovation in transportation, and demonstrates it with a short use case centered on Uber-related tweets around a protest in Portugal. Strengths: the topic is relevant to smart cities and transportation policy; the architecture is sensible (data collection, entity recognition/disambiguation, sentiment analysis, analytics); and the Uber use case is intuitive and helps illustrate the pipeline's potential. Weaknesses and areas for improvement: (1) The methodological description is often vague and at times internally inconsistent — the paper alternately refers to lexicon-based sentiment analysis and to supervised classifiers enriched with word embeddings, without clearly specifying which approach was used in the Uber experiment. (2) There is essentially no quantitative evaluation of the proposed components: no classifier performance metrics (precision/recall/F1), no error analysis for entity disambiguation, and no baseline comparisons to existing systems. (3) The use case dataset is very small and narrowly focused (two days, ~400 tweets), limiting confidence in the generality of the conclusions. (4) Prior work comparison is sparse: the paper cites several related systems but does not meaningfully compare capabilities or results. (5) Presentation issues: figures are referenced but not shown in the provided text; there are duplicated/garbled passages and some typos that impede readability. (6) Important practical concerns—privacy, ethical considerations, and reproducibility (availability of code/data)—are not discussed. Overall, the paper reads as a description of a useful applied prototype rather than as a mature empirical study. I recommend strengthening the manuscript by (a) clarifying the exact algorithms used at each pipeline stage, (b) providing quantitative evaluation (annotated datasets, classifier and NER/NERD metrics, error analysis), (c) comparing with baseline or prior systems, (d) expanding the empirical study to larger/more diverse datasets and longer time windows, and (e) improving presentation and reproducibility (release code/data or implementation details). With these improvements the work would be a more convincing contribution to the literature on leveraging social media for transportation innovation.
